question: Compare the primary coolant flow paths during normal operation vs DHRS operation.

nuscale_ch05_0025.title: References
nuscale_ch05_0025.summary: This section details the materials used in the NuScale reactor pressurizer, specifying the alloys and standards for each component, such as the baffle plate, heater bundles, cover plate, and bolting. It emphasizes the use of corrosion-resistant materials in accordance with ASME BPVC Section II specifications to ensure compatibility and durability in the reactor coolant system.

nuscale_ch05_0024.title: Pressurizer
nuscale_ch05_0024.summary: The pressurizer, integrated into the upper region of the reactor vessel and separated by a baffle plate, regulates Reactor Coolant System (RCS) pressure by maintaining a saturated steam-water interface. It uses heaters and spray flow to control pressure and is designed to handle operating transients without triggering reactor trips or safety system actuations, with instrumentation providing continuous monitoring and control.

nuscale_ch05_0006.title: System Evaluation
nuscale_ch05_0006.summary: The System Evaluation section describes how the NuScale Power Plant optimizes performance across various power levels by establishing steady-state parameters such as coolant temperatures, flow rates, and pressures, using both software and manual calculations. It accounts for uncertainties in flow resistance and core bypass, ensuring low pressure losses to support natural circulation, and confirms that certain uncertainties, like SG tube plugging, do not affect primary coolant flow rate determination.

nuscale_ch05_0022.title: Decay Heat Removal System
nuscale_ch05_0022.summary: The Decay Heat Removal System (DHRS) is an engineered safety feature designed to remove residual and core decay heat from the reactor after shutdown, especially when normal cooling is unavailable, without relying on external power. It ensures the reactor coolant system (RCS) temperature drops below 420°F within 36 hours, maintaining fuel and pressure boundary safety limits, and its reliability and risk significance are rigorously evaluated. The DHRS operates independently of the Emergency Core Cooling System (ECCS), but both can function together if needed.

nuscale_ch05_0002.title: Summary Description
nuscale_ch05_0002.summary: The NuScale reactor coolant system (RCS) uses natural circulation, eliminating the need for pumps or external piping, and is integrated within each NuScale Power Module. The RCS is designed to safely transfer heat from the reactor core, maintain proper coolant chemistry, and ensure uniform boron concentration, supporting both normal and off-normal plant conditions.

nuscale_ch05_0005.title: System Components
nuscale_ch05_0005.summary: This section describes the main components of the NuScale reactor system, including the Reactor Pressure Vessel (RPV), Reactor Coolant System (RCS) piping, and the Pressurizer (PZR). The RPV serves as a primary barrier to fission product release and supports key reactor internals, while the RCS piping connects various subsystems, and the PZR is integrated into the RPV to help control system pressure.

nuscale_ch05_0020.title: Steam Generators
nuscale_ch05_0020.summary: Each NuScale Power Module’s reactor pressure vessel contains two integral steam generators, which are part of the reactor coolant system and share the vessel’s pressure boundary. The steam generator system includes feedwater and main steam piping, thermal relief valves, and access ports, with the reactor pressure vessel itself forming the shell and pressure boundary for the steam generators. The design ensures separation between the primary and secondary sides, with specific components forming the reactor coolant pressure boundary.

nuscale_ch05_0023.title: Reactor Coolant System High-Point Vents
nuscale_ch05_0023.summary: This section outlines the testing and inspection requirements for the DHRS (Decay Heat Removal System) components in the NuScale reactor, specifying compliance with ASME codes for preservice and inservice inspections. It details the classification and testing procedures for DHRS actuation valves, including full-stroke exercising during cold shutdown conditions and position verification, as well as a one-time in-situ test of the DHRS function during initial startup.

nuscale_ch05_0003.title: Design Basis
nuscale_ch05_0003.summary: The Design Basis section describes the NuScale reactor coolant system (RCS), which uses natural circulation without pumps or external piping to transfer heat from the reactor core to the steam generator. The RCS is designed to ensure safe and effective cooling, maintain water chemistry, and provide uniform boron concentration under all operating conditions, supporting both performance and safety requirements.

nuscale_ch05_0009.title: Overpressure Protection
nuscale_ch05_0009.summary: This section outlines NuScale's compliance with overpressure protection requirements by referencing applicable ASME Boiler and Pressure Vessel Code (BPVC) and Operations and Maintenance (OM) Code cases, as mandated by various sections of 10 CFR 50.55a and related Regulatory Guides (RGs). It details how code cases for design, fabrication, construction, preservice, and inservice inspections and testing are selected and verified to meet NRC requirements, with a consolidated list provided in Table 5.2-1.

edges.description: Section A references Section B by citing its detailed descriptions of the pressurizer spray nozzles and related components, indicating that Section B provides foundational information for the material specifications discussed in Section A.

Section A provides detailed component descriptions of the pressurizer within the reactor coolant system, while Section B discusses the system-level evaluation of coolant flow paths and heat removal, connecting them through their shared focus on primary coolant system components and their roles during different operational modes.

Section A details the components and materials of the pressurizer, which is part of the primary coolant flow path during normal operation, while Section B describes the DHRS, which provides an alternate flow path for removing decay heat when normal operation is not possible; both sections are connected by their focus on different flow paths within the reactor coolant system.

Section A provides detailed component and material information about the pressurizer, which is one of the RCS components generally described in Section B's summary of the reactor coolant system and its reliance on natural circulation.

Section A details the materials and components of the pressurizer, which is referenced in Section B's description of operational steps involving the pressurizer during DHRS operation, specifically regarding water level and pressure management.

Section A describes the pressurizer's role in maintaining pressure equilibrium in the reactor coolant system during normal operation, while Section B discusses the use of the steam generators and DHRS for decay heat removal, both of which involve different primary coolant flow paths depending on the operational mode.

Section A describes the pressurizer's role in the reactor coolant system during normal operation, while Section B outlines the DHRS's function in providing an alternative coolant flow path for decay heat removal when normal operation is not possible, thus both sections detail different coolant flow paths relevant to the analysis question.

Section A describes the pressurizer's integration and flow path with the reactor coolant system, while Section B provides a general overview of the reactor coolant system's components and natural circulation flow, connecting them by specifying that the pressurizer (from A) is part of the RCS (from B) and involved in primary coolant flow paths.

Section A describes the pressurizer's role and flow path connection to the reactor coolant system during normal operation, while Section B outlines how the pressurizer's water level and pressure are managed and altered during DHRS operation, connecting both sections through the operational changes in coolant flow and pressurizer function.

Section A describes how the DHRS connects to the steam and feed piping to remove decay heat from the primary coolant, while Section B details the DHRS's function to remove post-trip decay heat when normal cooling is unavailable, thus both sections explain different operational flow paths for primary coolant under normal and DHRS conditions.

Section A discusses how the DHRS connects to the steam and feed piping to remove decay heat from the primary coolant, while Section B describes the normal operation of the reactor coolant system (RCS) relying on natural circulation without pumps, thus both sections detail different primary coolant flow paths under normal and DHRS operation.

Section A explains that the DHRS uses the steam generator (SG) connections to remove decay heat from the primary coolant, while Section B details the operational steps (such as isolating the SGs and recirculating feedwater through the DHRS heat exchangers) that change the primary coolant flow path during DHRS operation compared to normal operation.

Section B describes the normal operation of the primary coolant flow path via natural circulation in the RCS, while Section A describes the alternate flow path and function of the DHRS during events when normal secondary-side cooling is unavailable, thus providing the basis for comparison between normal and DHRS operation.

Section A describes the function and design basis of the DHRS for removing decay heat when normal cooling is unavailable, while Section B outlines the operational steps and component configurations (such as isolation of SGs and recirculation through DHRS heat exchangers) that establish the actual coolant flow paths during DHRS operation, thus providing specific operational details that implement the general requirements described in Section A.

Section A provides a general overview of the reactor coolant system's reliance on natural circulation during normal operation, while Section B details specific component operations and flow path changes that occur during DHRS operation, connecting the two by describing how the coolant flow path is altered between these modes.

Section A describes the components and subsystems within the reactor pressure vessel, including both the steam generators and the decay heat removal system (DHRS), which are directly involved in defining the primary coolant flow paths during both normal and DHRS operation, while Section B provides detailed material and design information about the pressurizer, a component also housed within the RPV and referenced in Section A.

Section B describes the pressurizer's location and its low-resistance flow path to the reactor coolant system, which is relevant to Section A's overview of the reactor coolant system components (including the pressurizer and steam generators) that define the primary coolant flow paths during both normal and DHRS operation.

Section B explains that the DHRS uses the steam generators (described in Section A) to remove decay heat from the primary coolant, directly connecting the function of the SGs in both normal and DHRS operation.

Section A discusses inspection and classification requirements for DHRS components, while Section B describes the materials and components of the pressurizer, with both sections referencing specific reactor coolant system components relevant to different coolant flow paths.

Section A details inspection and classification requirements for DHRS components, including actuation valves, while Section B describes how the DHRS connects to the steam and feed piping to remove decay heat from the primary coolant, thus both sections are related through their focus on DHRS components and their roles in the primary coolant flow path during different operational modes.

Section A provides a general overview of the reactor coolant system and its reliance on natural circulation, while Section B specifies detailed component information (such as the pressurizer and its materials) that supports the system described in Section A.

Section A describes the overall reactor coolant system and its reliance on natural circulation, while Section B provides specific details about the pressurizer's integration and its flow path connection to the RCS, clarifying how pressure and coolant flow are managed between these regions.

Section A describes the primary coolant flow path during normal operation via natural circulation within the reactor coolant system, while Section B explains how the DHRS connects to the steam generator piping to remove decay heat from the primary coolant during DHRS operation, thus specifying the different flow paths under each mode.

Section A describes the components and flow paths of the reactor coolant system, including both the steam generators (normal operation) and the DHRS (decay heat removal system), while Section B details inspection and classification requirements for DHRS components, thus Section B provides regulatory specifications for the DHRS elements mentioned in Section A.

Section A details the components within the reactor pressure vessel, including steam generators and the decay heat removal system, while Section B provides a general overview of the reactor coolant system's reliance on natural circulation, connecting both sections through their descriptions of primary coolant flow paths and system components.

Section A specifies inspection and classification requirements for DHRS components and actuation valves, which are part of the alternate coolant flow path during DHRS operation, while Section B provides a general description of the primary coolant flow path during normal operation, thus Section A provides details for the specific components referenced generally in Section B.
Section B references Section A for material descriptions of the pressurizer components, specifically pointing to Section 5.2 for details on the RPV upper shell and upper head materials.

Section A discusses regulatory compliance and overpressure protection requirements for the reactor coolant boundary, while Section B describes the physical design and function of the pressurizer, which is a component relevant to maintaining pressure and thus overpressure protection in the reactor coolant system.

Section A discusses regulatory compliance for reactor coolant boundary integrity, while Section B describes the operational flow paths and performance criteria of the primary coolant system, connecting them through the design and operational requirements that ensure safe coolant flow during both normal and DHRS operation.
